name: Avenc de sa Coma Fosca
equipment: 1 rope of 25m, 2 slings, 1 protector, rope or sling of 16m for deviation
description: A small 13.5m deep chasm with two access openings, featuring a collapse-type entrance hall (11x14.5x8m) filled with debris, large stalactites, and animal bone remains. The cave contains beautiful parietal flowstones, botryoidal speleothems with fine coralloids, and a significant stalagmitic formation. A hidden second chamber (Sala Amagada) is beautifully decorated with columns, eccentric formations, fistular stalactites, and a dry gour.
full_description:
Description

A small chasm of 13.5 m in depth located at the head of sa Coma Fosca, with two access mouths. The larger one exceeds three meters in length by one in width, while the smaller one, slightly over a meter deep, gives way to a small ramp with considerable slope, from where the floor is reached after an 8 m free-hanging section. The orientation is N-S.

Entrance Hall (Sala de S'Entrada)

Whether we use one entrance or the other, upon reaching the floor we will land on a pile of debris where abundant blocks of different sizes accumulate, stalactites — some of great size — as well as bone remains of both caprines and canids. The hall is of the collapse type, with a more or less oval shape and dimensions of 11 x 14.5 x 8 m.

The reconstructive processes of the hall are abundant, with beautiful parietal flowstones standing out, botryoidal speleothems on which very fine coralloids develop, as well as considerable ceiling ornamentation, in addition to the stalagmitic formation located right below the entrance.

In the deepest zone of the hall, and therefore of the cave, there is a sinkhole where the marks left by water on its walls are clearly visible, at a height of approximately one meter, and where continuity may exist.

Hidden Hall (Sala Amagada)

There are two accesses to this hall. One is located right above the sinkhole of the Entrance Hall; the other is accessed a little further to the right after climbing a couple of meters to reach a narrow opening where clearing work was carried out. This access is very narrow and not suitable for everyone. The dimensions of the hall are 4 x 4.5 x 2 m and it is beautifully concretioned, to the point that it is difficult to move through it without risking breaking some of its speleothems, among which stand out a large quantity of columns, stalactites, eccentric formations, and fistular stalactites. Stalagmites are also present, as well as a beautiful dry gour whose interior is filled with a large quantity of stalactite fragments. The cornices of the gour are very well developed and in good condition.
